retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Interossei Muscles -- Structure and Function -- Dorsal Interossei
retrieved_docs.content: The 1st dorsal interosseous originates from the adjacent surfaces of the 1st and 2nd metacarpals and inserts into the lateral base of the 2nd proximal phalanx and the extensor hood. The 2nd dorsal interosseous originates from the medial aspect of the 2nd metacarpal and the lateral aspect of the 3rd metacarpal. This muscle inserts into the lateral base of the 3rd proximal phalanx and its extensor hood.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Interossei Muscles -- Structure and Function -- Dorsal Interossei
retrieved_docs.content: Among all intrinsic hand muscles, the dorsal interossei are the most dorsally located (see Image . Dorsal Interossei of the Hand). These bipennate muscles serve as the primary abductors of the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th digits at the MCP joints (see Image . Dorsal Interossei on Magnetic Resonance Imaging). The dorsal interossei also contribute to MCP joint flexion and extension at the PIP and DIP joints. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Interossei Muscles -- Structure and Function -- Dorsal Interossei
retrieved_docs.content: The 3rd dorsal interosseous originates from the medial surface of the 3rd metacarpal and the lateral surface of the 4th metacarpal, inserting into the medial base of the 3rd proximal phalanx and the extensor hood of the 3rd digit. The 4th dorsal interosseous originates from the lateral aspect of the 4th metacarpal and the medial side of the 5th metacarpal. This muscle inserts into the lateral base of the 4th proximal phalanx and the extensor hood of the 4th digit. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Interossei Muscles -- Structure and Function -- Palmar Interossei
retrieved_docs.content: The palmar (volar) interossei are unipennate muscles originating from the metacarpals of the respective digit to which they are associated (see Image . Palmar Interossei of the Hand). These muscles adduct the 1st, 2nd, 4th, and 5th digits about an axis through the midline of the 3rd digit. Adduction occurs at the MCP joints. Additionally, the palmar interossei contribute to flexion at the MCP joints and extension at the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) and distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints of their respective digits.

retrieved_docs.title: Wrist Imaging -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: Central nervous system communication derives from the median, radial, and ulnar nerves. [20] The median nerve is responsible for sensation to the volar aspect of palm and first three digits (thumb, index, middle) as well as the radial half of the 4th (ring). It also innervates the dorsal aspect of these digits from the proximal interphalangeal joints (PIP) through the tips. The median nerve and its branches are responsible for motor innervation to pronator teres, flexor carpi radialis, palmaris longus, flexor digitorum superficialis, flexor digitorum profundus, flexor pollicis longus, pronator quadratus, abductor pollicis brevis, flexor pollicis brevis, opponens pollicis, and the first and second lumbricals. [21] [22] The ulnar nerve supplies sensation to the volar and dorsal aspect of the 5th digit (little) and the ulnar portion of the 4th. [23] Motor innervation by the ulnar nerve includes flexor carpi ulnaris, flexor digitorum profundus, palmar brevis, abductor digiti minimi, flexor digiti minimi, opponens digiti minimi, 3rd and 4th lumbricals, and palmar/dorsal interosseous muscles. The radial nerve supplies sensation to the dorsal aspect of the thumb and wrist, as well as the 2nd to 3rd digits to the level of the PIP. It also innervates the radial aspect of the 4th to the level of the PIP on the dorsal side.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Intrinsic Muscles -- Structure and Function -- Interossei
retrieved_docs.content: The interossei consist of 4 dorsal and 3 palmar interossei, located between the metacarpals. A commonly used mnemonic to recall their actions is “DAB and PAD.” [3] “DAB” refers to the dorsal interossei, which abduct the digits, while “PAD” refers to the palmar interossei, which adduct the digits. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Interossei Muscles -- Structure and Function -- Palmar Interossei
retrieved_docs.content: The 2nd palmar interosseous originates from the medial surface of the base of the 2nd metacarpal and inserts into the medial portion of the extensor hood and base of the proximal 1st phalanx. The 3rd and 4th palmar interossei originate from the lateral aspects of the 4th and 5th metacarpals and insert into the lateral portions of their respective extensor hoods and the bases of their corresponding proximal phalanges.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Dorsal Interossei Muscle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The 3rd dorsal interosseous muscle originates from the medial portion of the 3rd metacarpal and lateral portion of the 4th metacarpal, with insertion at the medial base of the 3rd phalanx and extensor hood of the 3rd digit. The 4th dorsal interosseous muscle arises from the lateral aspect of the 4th metacarpal and medial side of the 5th metacarpal, inserting into the lateral base of the 4th phalanx and extensor hood of the 4th digit.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Dorsal Interossei Muscle -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Classically, students are taught that all dorsal interossei have 2 heads, termed "bipennate." However, a retrospective study found that approximately 25% of dorsal interossei are unipennate. [10] The study also concluded that traditional teachings have oversimplified the attachment sites of both palmar and dorsal interossei, which exhibit significant variability. [11] These distal attachment sites include bony attachments, the volar plate, and portions of the extensor hood.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Dorsal Interossei Muscle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The 1st dorsal interosseous muscle originates from the adjacent surfaces of the 1st and 2nd metacarpals and inserts into the lateral base of the 2nd phalanx and extensor hood of the 2nd digit. [2] The 2nd dorsal interosseous muscle arises from the medial aspect of the 2nd metacarpal and lateral aspect of the 3rd metacarpal, inserting into the lateral base of the 3rd phalanx and corresponding extensor hood.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Dorsal Interossei Muscle -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The dorsal interosseous muscles are paired intrinsic muscles of the hand situated between the metacarpals (see Image . Dorsal Interossei of the Hand). This group consists of 4 muscles responsible for finger abduction. These muscles also contribute to flexion at the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints and extension at the interphalangeal joints. All interossei receive innervation from the deep branch of the ulnar nerve. Injury to this nerve can significantly impair intrinsic hand function, particularly finger abduction and adduction, which depend primarily on the interossei. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Intrinsic Muscles -- Structure and Function -- Interossei
retrieved_docs.content: The dorsal interossei abduct the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th digits. These muscles originate from adjacent sides of the metacarpals and insert on the extensor hood and proximal phalanges of the corresponding digits. The palmar interossei adduct the 2nd, 4th, and 5th digits. These muscles arise from the palmar surfaces of the corresponding metacarpals and insert on the extensor hood and proximal phalanges. All interossei receive innervation from the deep branch of the ulnar nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Dorsal Interossei Muscle -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Dorsal Interossei Muscle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Among the intrinsic hand muscles, the dorsal interossei occupy the most dorsal position (see Image . Dorsal Interossei on Magnetic Resonance Imaging). These bipennate muscles serve as primary abductors of the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th digits at the MCP joints and assist with flexion at the MCP joints and extension at the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) and distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Muscles -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The interossei muscles act by adducting and abducting the fingers. The mnemonic PAD and DAB helps to remember the actions of each muscle. The "P" in PAD stands for the palmar interossei . Interossei muscles on the palmar surface adduct the fingers, bringing them towards the midline. The "D" in DAB stands for dorsal interossei . The dorsally placed interossei permit abduction of the phalanges, moving them away from the midline. The second and third letters of PAD and DAB stand for the direction of finger movement. The dorsal interossei originate from the adjacent sides of two metacarpals. They insert at the extensor hood and proximal phalanx of each phalange. There are three palmar interossei, which each originate at the medial or lateral surface of the 2nd, 4th, and 5th metacarpals and insert at the base of digits 2-4 and the extensor hood of each finger. [4] These muscles receive innervation from the deep branch of the ulnar nerve. The dorsal interossei are bipennate, and the palmar interossei are unipennate.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Dorsal Interossei Muscle -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on which nerve fibers are affected, patients may experience weakness in finger abduction. While the lumbricals are the primary contributors to flexion at the MCP joints and extension at the DIP and PIP joints, the interossei also play a minor role in these movements. A late manifestation of ulnar nerve injury, the ulnar claw hand deformity, results from weakness in the 3rd and 4th lumbricals, as well as the interossei. This condition causes weakness in the extension of the 4th and 5th MCP joints and flexion of the 4th and 5th PIP and DIP joints. [16]

Together, these entities and relationships illustrate a structured framework that assists clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic, surgical, or injection‑based treatments for tendon and tendon‑sheath conditions of the hand and other musculoskeletal sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1914
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration of fascia of hand is a medical procedure performed on the musculoskeletal system, specifically targeting the fascial tissue of the hand for removal of fluid or other material. This intervention is classified as a procedure on the musculoskeletal system, reflecting its role in diagnosing or treating conditions affecting hand fascia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1915
retrieved_docs.content: The division of joint capsule is an anatomical concept that encompasses specific subdivisions of the joint capsule in different regions of the upper extremity. In particular, the division of joint capsule of hand represents the hand‑specific portion of this structure, while the division of joint capsule of wrist denotes the wrist‑specific portion. Both the division of joint capsule of hand and the division of joint capsule of wrist are recognized as distinct subclasses of the broader division of joint capsule, reflecting the specialized anatomy of the joint capsule in these adjacent but functionally separate joints. Understanding these regional divisions aids clinicians in accurately describing joint anatomy, diagnosing joint‑related pathologies, and planning targeted interventions for the hand and wrist.

This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and therapeutic planning, especially the central role of nerve decompression procedures across multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Release of tendon sheath of hand is classified as a type of hand surgery (العمليات الجراحية في اليد).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: Hand and wrist incisional surgeries are organized under the “Hand, Wrist: Incision” category, which is nested within both “other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” and “OPERATIVE PROCEDURES ON THE UPPER EXTREMITY: WRIST AND HAND.” Specific procedures in this group include aspiration of the bursa of hand, aspiration of fascia of hand, division of the joint capsule of hand, division of the joint capsule of wrist, drainage of tendon of hand, puncture of the bursa of hand, release for tenosynovitis of the abductor pollicis longus, release for tenosynovitis of the extensor (external) pollicis brevis, and release of the tendon sheath of the hand. Aspiration of the bursa of hand is also classified under “Injections and aspirations of muscles; tendons; bursa; joints and soft tissue,” reflecting its minimally invasive fluid‑removal role, while aspiration of fascia of hand is listed as a “Procedure on musculoskeletal system,” emphasizing its musculoskeletal operative relevance. The

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1913
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration of bursa of hand is a medical procedure that is classified within the broader category of injections and aspirations of muscles, tendons, bursa, joints, and soft tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: of tendon sheath fall under the broader category of other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons. “Release of tendon sheath of hand” is mapped from this same therapeutic group and is recognized as a type of hand surgical operation (العمليات الجراحية في اليد). The procedure “Removal of rice bodies from tendon sheaths of hand” is related to, and possibly synonymous with, the broader “Other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” group. Collectively, these entities illustrate a structured framework that links specific aspiration, diagnostic, exploratory, irrigation, release, and removal actions to their respective procedural categories, anatomical sites, and linguistic equivalents, facilitating coherent understanding and management of musculoskeletal procedures in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: Removal of rice bodies from tendon sheaths of the hand is regarded as a therapeutic intervention that is closely related to, and possibly synonymous with, other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons. The broader category “other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” functions as a classification that encompasses such specific interventions, including the removal of rice bodies from hand tendon sheaths. This relationship indicates that the hand‑specific procedure is considered part of the general group of muscle and tendon therapies, reflecting its inclusion within the overall taxonomy of musculoskeletal therapeutic techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1906
retrieved_docs.content: The head of the first metatarsal bone is a distinct anatomical region of the foot that is defined by laterality, indicating that it can be identified as belonging to either the left or right side.

In a parallel branch, “Operation on bursa” functions as a general category that includes the more specific “Puncture of bursa of hand,” illustrating an inverse‑is‑a relationship where the puncture is a particular form of bursal operation. Collectively, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy of diagnostic and therapeutic procedures on muscles, tendons, tendon sheaths and bursae, connecting individual hand‑focused surgeries to their respective broader procedural families and supporting organized clinical documentation and patient‑care planning.
